Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings after my extracurriculars, but I found myself struggling to actually focus when I sat down. I'd put in about 18 hours a week, but a lot of that time felt unproductive because my mind kept wandering. I learn best by listening, so I'd often play lecture recordings while doing other things, hoping something would stick.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what else was going on. I had extracurriculars that took up significant chunks of my week, so I'd cram studying around those commitments. When deadlines approached, I'd suddenly try to catch up, but by then I was already behind. There wasn't really a consistent weekly rhythm—more like bursts of activity followed by periods where I just couldn't bring myself to engage.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about something due would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, especially when I was feeling anxious about falling behind. The gamified tasks actually helped a bit—seeing points or badges gave me a small push to at least open the platform. But without that external prompt, I'd often forget to check in.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, logging into the LMS did become somewhat routine for me. The gamified elements made it feel less like a chore and more like something I could accomplish. But then when assignments started piling up and I couldn't complete them properly, that routine fell apart. The sadness I was feeling made it hard to maintain any consistent habits.
Q5: The recorded lectures were genuinely helpful because I could listen to them multiple times. As an auditory learner, hearing explanations worked way better for me than reading dense text. The discussion forums were also useful when other students explained concepts in different ways—sometimes their words clicked better than the instructor's. I appreciated resources I could engage with through listening rather than just reading.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They required a lot of focused attention that I just couldn't muster with everything going on. The lengthy written assignments felt overwhelming, so I'd delay starting them until it was almost too late. I know my assignment completion rate suffered because of this—I just couldn't push through the mental block.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it connected to something I actually understood. The gamified tasks were great for this because completing them gave me instant feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from what I already knew, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to feel like I was actually learning, not just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a relief honestly—no pressure meant I could engage with them more freely. Required activities carried this weight of expectation that made them feel heavier, especially when I was already stressed. Sometimes I'd actually enjoy optional content more because I chose to do it. But when I was really struggling, even optional things felt like too much effort.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used YouTube videos from other educators when the course material wasn't clicking. My friends and I would sometimes study together in person, quizzing each other verbally, which suited my learning style much better. None of that showed up in the LMS.
Q10: There were many times when I'd listen to downloaded lectures repeatedly while walking to class or before bed. I'd also have long conversations with classmates about course concepts that helped me understand things better. Sometimes I'd sit with my notes and talk through problems out loud to myself—it sounds weird but it helped me process information. The LMS couldn't capture any of that learning.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download lecture recordings once and then listen to them multiple times offline throughout the week. It was more convenient than logging in every time, plus I could listen without needing internet access. My watch time on the LMS probably looked low, but I was actually engaging with that content repeatedly in my own way.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. I was feeling really sad and overwhelmed, and my motivation just tanked. The stress was building up but I couldn't seem to convert that into productive action. My extracurriculars were demanding more time, and I started to feel like I was failing at everything. That's when my engagement really dropped off.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from my other commitments, logging into the LMS felt like climbing a mountain. The confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections entirely because I didn't want to feel stupid. Competing responsibilities meant I was always triaging, and unfortunately, this course often lost that battle. The fatigue made everything feel twice as hard.
Q14: There were definitely times when I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about other things or just feeling too sad to focus. I'd technically be 'studying' but nothing was sinking in. It's frustrating because the time was spent but the learning wasn't happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The combination of low motivation and feeling overwhelmed made starting feel impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt better, but that better moment rarely came. My assignment completion rate really suffered because of this avoidance pattern. I know my resilience usually helps me bounce back, but this semester was different.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd have these bursts of frantic activity right before something was due. The problem was that by then, I didn't have enough time to do quality work. My exam score and final grade really reflect that last-minute approach—I knew the material somewhat but couldn't demonstrate it properly under pressure.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to keep trying, but critical feedback sometimes made me feel worse about myself. I'd avoid checking grades when I was already feeling low because I didn't want to add to the sadness. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal and more like a game.
Q18: The course structure was actually pretty demanding with nine online courses to juggle. Having so many modules and deadlines made it hard to prioritize this particular course. The structure didn't naturally accommodate my auditory learning style—there was a lot of reading required. I think if the course had been more audio-focused, I would have engaged more consistently.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they gave me small wins when I needed them. But the heavy reliance on written assignments made things harder for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums were helpful when they were active, but sometimes they felt dead. Having more podcast-style content or verbal explanations would have suited me better.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat, actually. I was logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions, but my actual assignment submissions were really low. For me, it was about not being able to translate engagement into completed work. The depression and low motivation created this gap between consuming content and producing output. Understanding something and proving you understand it are two different challenges.
Q21: That makes sense to me because a lot of real learning happens outside the platform. Someone could be studying effectively with downloaded materials or study groups and barely touch the LMS. My peer interactions and discussion posts were decent, but my actual performance didn't reflect that. Sometimes the LMS just isn't where the deep learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you were actually paying attention. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with friends, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture your emotional state—whether you were engaged or just going through the motions. The numbers tell a story, but it's an incomplete one.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and curious. For me, real engagement feels like when I'm listening to something and my brain is actively making connections. This semester, I wanted to be engaged but often couldn't get there emotionally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite feeling sad and unmotivated. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures offline or discussing concepts with friends. It also wouldn't show that I genuinely cared about learning but was struggling with my mental state. My high resilience and improved mood after the gamified intervention gave me hope, but the data just shows my failures, not my fight.
